question: Did the review adhere to pre-defined objectives and eligibility criteria?
answers: nan

question: Were the eligibility crieria appropriate for the review question?
answers:
Articles were considered eligible if (1) they included
peer-reviewed data from an observational study; (2) the
criteria for confirmation of the HZ diagnosis in our included
studies were as follows: (1) physician's diagnosis
of HZ with clinical symptoms (vesicular rash or dermatomal
pain or others); (2) serological analysis (dried blood
spot analysis) or laboratory tests (Tzanck smear or viral
culture or polymerase chain reaction); and (3) HZ-related
diagnostic codes with the International Classification of
Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10: B02), or Ninth Revision
(ICD-9: 053). Only the cases satisfied with the above 3
criteria in the meantime were included in our studies. Thus,
those cases with vesicle symptoms not caused by VZV
infection were excluded in a stringent manner. Articles
were also considered eligible if (3) stroke was diagnosed
with clinical information and radiological investigation.
Stroke was identified using diagnostic codes with ICD-
10 I60-64 and G45 or ICD-9 430-438; (4) concerned the
risk of stroke/TIA or MI after HZ infection

question: Were eligibility criteria unambiguous?
answers:
Articles were considered eligible if (1) they included
peer-reviewed data from an observational study; (2) the
criteria for confirmation of the HZ diagnosis in our included
studies were as follows: (1) physician's diagnosis
of HZ with clinical symptoms (vesicular rash or dermatomal
pain or others); (2) serological analysis (dried blood
spot analysis) or laboratory tests (Tzanck smear or viral
culture or polymerase chain reaction); and (3) HZ-related
diagnostic codes with the International Classification of
Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10: B02), or Ninth Revision
(ICD-9: 053). Only the cases satisfied with the above 3
criteria in the meantime were included in our studies. Thus,
those cases with vesicle symptoms not caused by VZV
infection were excluded in a stringent manner. Articles
were also considered eligible if (3) stroke was diagnosed
with clinical information and radiological investigation.
Stroke was identified using diagnostic codes with ICD-
10 I60-64 and G45 or ICD-9 430-438; (4) concerned the
risk of stroke/TIA or MI after HZ infection and (5) reported
multivariate-adjusted relative risks (RRs) and 95%
confidence intervals (CIs)

question: Were all restrictions in eligibility crieria based on study characteristics appropriate (e.g. date, sample size, study quality, outcomes measured)? If yes, indicate which study characteristic was an inclusion/exclusion criteria
answers:
Articles were considered eligible if (1) they included peer-reviewed data from an observational study; (2) the criteria for confirmation of the HZ diagnosis in our included studies were as follows: (1) physician's diagnosis of HZ with clinical symptoms (vesicular rash or dermatomal pain or others); (2) serological analysis (dried blood spot analysis) or laboratory tests (Tzanck smear or viral culture or polymerase chain reaction); and (3) HZ-related diagnostic codes with the International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10: B02), or Ninth Revision (ICD-9: 053). Only the cases satisfied with the above 3
criteria in the meantime were included in our studies. Thus, those cases with vesicle symptoms not caused by VZV infection were excluded in a stringent manner. Articles were also considered eligible if (3) stroke was diagnosed with clinical information and radiological investigation. Stroke was identified using diagnostic codes with ICD- 10 I60-64 and G45 or ICD-9 430-438; (4) concerned the risk of stroke/TIA or MI after HZ infection; and (5) reported multivariate-adjusted relative risks (RRs) and 95% confidence intervals (CIs).We excluded (1) studies of patients with a diagnosis of stroke before HZ diagnosis; (2) reviews and letters; (3) duplicate publications; (4) non- English language articles; and (5) data insufficient for calculating a risk estimate with 95% CIs, or unqualified data. When 2 articles appeared to report results with overlapping data, only the data representing the most recent publication or with the larger sample size were included in the meta-analysis. No publications were excluded on the basis of quality, sample size, or other objective criteria relevant to study design and analysis.

question: Were methods additional to database searching used to identify relevant reports?
answers:
Reference lists from related main studies and review
articles were checked for additional relevant reports.

question: Were the terms and structure of the search strategy likely to retrieve as many eligible studies as possible?
answers:
Two authors (Y.Z. and G.L.) independently searched PubMed for articles published in English up to October 20, 2016, by using the key words ("Stroke"[Mesh] OR Stroke[tiab] OR ischemic stroke[tiab] OR ischaemic stroke[tiab] OR non-ischemic stroke[tiab] OR hemorrhagic stroke[tiab] OR poststroke[tiab] OR apoplexy[tiab] OR cerebral arterial disease[tiab] OR cerebrovascular accident[tiab] OR "Myocardial Infarction"[Mesh] OR myocardial infarction[tiab] OR myocardium infarction[tiab] OR myocardic infarction[tiab] OR "Ischemic Attack, Transient"[Mesh] OR transient ischaemic attack[tiab] OR transient ischemic attack[tiab] OR ischemic attack*[tiab]
OR ischaemic attack*[tiab] OR brain ischemi*[tiab] OR brain ischaemi*[tiab] OR transient brain ischemia[tiab] OR cerebral ischemia[tiab]), AND ("Herpes  Zoster"[Mesh] OR Herpes Zoster[tiab] OR Shingles[tiab] OR Zona[tiab] OR Zoster[tiab] OR VZV[tiab]).

question: Were search strategy restrictions based on date, publication format, or language appropriate?
answers: articles published in English up to October 20, 2016. We excluded (1) studies of patients with a diagnosis of stroke before HZ diagnosis; (2) reviews and letters; (3) duplicate publications; (4) non-English language articles; and (5) data insufficient for calculating a risk estimate with 95% CIs, or unqualified data.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in selection of studies?
answers:
Two authors (Y.Z. and G.L.) independently searched PubMed for articles published in English up to October 20, 2016. Titles, abstracts, full texts, and reference lists of all identified reports were reviewed
in duplicate by the 2 authors, and extracted articles were double-checked by K.L. Disagreements were resolved by discussion among the 3 authors (Y.Z., G.L., and K.L.).

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in data collection?
answers:
We extracted data on (1) publication details (first author's
name, year of publication, and study design); (2) baseline
characteristics of the study population (country, study period,
number of observation groups, age, follow-up time);
and (3) other details (infection types, endpoints). If these
data were absent, we sent an email to the authors. We
used the results of the original studies from multivariable
models with the most complete adjustment for potential
confounders.

question: Was risk of bias (or methodological quality) formally assessed using appropriate criteria?
answers:
We evaluated the quality of reports by the Newcastle-
Ottawa quality assessment scale26 (
programs/clinical_epidemiology/nosgen.pdf). Two
independent reviewers read and scored all included studies.

question: Were efforts made to minimise error in risk of bias assessment?
answers: We evaluated the quality of reports by the Newcastle-Ottawa quality assessment scale26 ( Two independent reviewers read and scored all included studies. The Newcastle-Ottawa scale contains 8 items, 7 applicable to our study question. Items were classified into 3 domains: selection, comparability, and outcome. The total scores of 0-3, 4-6, and 7-9 stars were assigned for low, moderate, and high qualities, respectively. Any discrepancies between the 2 reviewers were resolved by joint reassessment until consensus was reached.

question: Did the synthesis include all studies that it should?
answers: We identified 392 relevant studies in the literature search; 20 were removed as duplicates and 331 were excluded on the basis of titles and abstracts. After manually screening the full text of 41 articles, 30 articles were excluded. Finally, our analysis included 11 studies.  Figure 1. Flow diagram of the search result for the meta-analysis.

question: Were all pre-defined analyses reported or departures explained?
answers: nan

question: Was the synthesis appropriate given the nature and similarity in the research questions, study designs and outcomes across included studies?
answers:
With heterogeneity, the random effects model
(DerSimonian-Laird method) was used; otherwise, the
fixed effects model (Mantel-Haenszel method) was used.

question: Was between-study variation (heterogeneity) minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers:
we performed several subgroup analyses to lower the heterogeneity and have more robust and accessible results,
by investigating time since infection, sex, age, region, and
treatment

question: Were the findings robust, e.g. as demonstrated through funnel plot or sensitivity analyses?
answers:
Because of the high heterogeneity, we conducted a sensitivity analysis. Eliminating one study in each group did
not predominantly change the overall RR (Figs S1-S3), which indicates the reliability of our results, despite some high
heterogeneity. Publication bias was detected by Begg's and Egger's linear test and summarized in Table 3. Our sensitivity analysis revealed that all 3 overall analyses were robust. Publication bias analyses also revealed that our results were stable.

question: Were biases in primary studies minimal or addressed in the synthesis?
answers: Publication bias was detected by Begg's and Egger's linear test and summarized in Table 3. Neither test for the HZO infection and stroke association indicated publication bias, but both tests indicated publication bias for the HZ infection and MI association. However, the pooled point estimation and 95% CI did not change after the trimand-fill method (Table S2), so the meta-analysis results for HZ infection and MI remained stable despite publication bias. Our study has several limitations. First, most of our included studies used administrative data to identify HZ, which could suggest 5%-15% false-positive cases because the ICD code may not be accurate. However, we considered TIA as minor stroke and the code for TIA diagnosis was likely to be incorrect. Meanwhile, some silent strokes or MI may not have been recorded. Second, we inevitably observed the high heterogeneity in our studies because of the study design, methodological differences, geographic regions, confounding factors, and the inherent differences between individuals, although we used the random effects model. Third, we found publication bias in the HZO analysis, which may be related to the search strategy restricted to English language literature.

question: Did the interpretation of findings address all of the concerns identified in Domains 1 to 4?
answers:
all of our included articles were high quality and included large samples, which guaranteed the
degree of accuracy and validity in our meta-analysis. We performed several subgroup analyses to lower the heterogeneity and have more robust and accessible results, by investigating time since infection, sex, age, region, and treatment. We inevitably observed the high heterogeneity in our studies because of the study design, methodological differences, geographic regions, confounding factors, and the inherent differences between individuals, although we used the random effects model. Third, we found publication bias in the HZO analysis, which may be related to the search strategy restricted to English language literature.

question: Was the relevance of identified studies to the review's research question appropriately considered?
answers: Table 1. Summary of basic characteristics. This meta-analysis of 11 observational studies demonstrated that risk of stroke/TIA was increased with HZ infection, even when controlling for multiple risk and confounding factors. The association of HZ infection and MI was less strong but still significant. We observed a strong effect for risk of stroke after HZO diagnosis, with a 1.9- fold increased risk.

question: Did the reviewers avoid emphasizing results on the basis of their statistical significance?
answers: Yes
